title: Writing Rules People Follow
subtitle: Why bad policies get ignored

hook.metaphor: A road sign that's a paragraph of legalese in tiny font, half-hidden behind a tree, won't stop anyone speeding — not because drivers are bad, but because the sign failed. Most 'policy compliance' problems are really *policy writing* problems: rules so long, vague, or buried that following them is harder than ignoring them.
hook.scenario: A 30-page security policy, dense with legal jargon and contradictions, is emailed once and never mentioned again. Staff don't follow it. Management blames the staff for non-compliance. Who's actually at fault here?

learn: The uncomfortable truth: **most non-compliance is a writing failure, not a people failure.** If a policy is unreadable, unclear, or impossible to find, people will default to whatever's easiest — and that's the policy's fault, not theirs (just like the buried road sign). Blaming staff (our management) misdiagnoses the problem.

Good policies are **clear and plain**: written for the actual reader, in plain language, short, and unambiguous. If the people who must follow a rule can't understand it in one read, it will be ignored — comprehension is a prerequisite for compliance.

They're **actionable**: they tell people what to actually *do* (or where to find the how), not just abstract principles. A policy that leaves the reader thinking 'okay… but what do I do?' has failed even if every word is technically correct.

They're **consistent and current**: free of contradictions with other policies, and kept up to date. Our 30-page contradictory document is unfollowable by design — when rules conflict, people stop trusting all of them.

And critically, they're **findable and digestible**: accessible where people work, searchable, and reasonable in length. A perfect policy nobody can locate or has time to read might as well not exist. The test of a policy isn't legal completeness — it's whether a busy human will actually read, understand, and act on it.
bridge: So at work, when a policy isn't being followed, you can resist the reflex to blame the people and look at the rule itself: *is it clear, short, actionable, consistent, and easy to find?* A policy is a communication tool — if it's written like impenetrable legalese and buried in an inbox, the failure is the writing, not the reader. Fix the sign before blaming the driver.

interactions.title: Whose fault is non-compliance?
interactions.prompt: A 30-page jargon-filled, contradictory security policy is ignored, and management blames the staff. Who's at fault?

options.label: The staff — they should read and follow policies.
options.feedback: Mostly the policy. Unreadable, contradictory, buried rules guarantee non-compliance regardless of staff intent. Blaming the reader misdiagnoses a writing failure.

options.label: The policy — unclear, too long, contradictory, and unfindable rules don't get followed.
options.feedback: Exactly. Most non-compliance is a writing/communication failure. Fix the sign (make it clear, short, actionable, findable) before blaming the driver.

options.label: Nobody — non-compliance is just inevitable.
options.feedback: It's not inevitable — it's caused by a badly written, badly distributed policy. A clear, findable, actionable version would be followed far more.

interactions.title: Followable or ignored-by-design?
interactions.prompt: Sort each policy trait by its effect on whether people actually comply.

items.label: Plain language a reader gets in one pass
Clear actions: 'do X, don't do Y'
Easy to find where people actually work
Emailed once and never seen again

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Most non-compliance is a writing failure, not a people failure — if a rule is unclear, too long, contradictory, or buried, people default to what's easy, so good policies are plain, actionable, consistent, and findable.”

checkpoint.q: Most policy non-compliance is actually caused by…
options: Policies that are unclear, too long, contradictory, or unfindable
checkpoint.explain: Like a buried, jargon-filled road sign, a bad policy fails the reader — fix the writing before blaming people.

options: As long and legalistic as possible
Clear, plain, actionable, consistent, and easy to find
checkpoint.explain: Comprehension is a prerequisite for compliance — a policy is a communication tool, not a legal brick.

checkpoint.q: The real test of a policy is whether…
options: A busy human will actually read, understand, and act on it
It is the longest in the company
checkpoint.explain: Legal completeness means nothing if no one reads or follows it — usability is what makes it work.
